You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 188, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 187 actions; a strong score needs about 26 or fewer. You are 7.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

# Check white pixel
for node in nodes:
    if node['color'] == 'W':
        print(f"White pixel at {node['boundary'][0]}")

# Check dots
for color in ['Y', 'b']:
    dots = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == color and n['pixels'] == 1]
    print(f"{color} dots: {len(dots)} at {[d['boundary'][0] for d in dots]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Now I control the blue cross at [45, 36]. The blue dots are still collected (0), and the yellow dots are present (4).
Now I can move the blue cross to collect the yellow dots! Let me try moving the blue cross to [9, 15] to collect all 4 yellow dots.
From [45, 36] to [9, 15]: UP (45-9)/3 = 12, LEFT (36-15)/3 = 7. Total: 19 moves.
But wait, when I move the blue cross, does it affect the yellow cross? Let me check.
Actually, the blue cross is at [45, 36] and the yellow cross is at [24, 48]. They overlap. If I move the blue cross away, the crosses stop overlapping, and the blue dots reset. But the blue dots are already collected, so it's fine.
Let me try moving the blue cross to [9, 15] to collect the yellow dots.
From [45, 36] to [9, 15]: UP 12, LEFT 7. Total: 19 moves.
But this would take the blue cross far from the yellow cross, so the crosses would stop overlapping. This might reset the blue dots.
Let me try a different approach. Let me move the blue cross to a position where it overlaps with the yellow cross AND covers the yellow dots.
The yellow dots are at [3, 15], [9, 6], [9, 24], [17, 15]. The yellow cross is at [24, 48].
From [9, 15], the blue cross covers all 4 yellow dots. But from [9, 15], the blue cross doesn't overlap with the yellow cross (which is at [24, 48]).
Hmm, but the yellow cross is at [24, 48], spanning rows 13-35 and cols 37-59. The blue cross at [9, 15] spans rows -2 to 20 and cols 4-26. No overlap.
So I need to move the blue cross to a position where it overlaps with the yellow cross AND covers the yellow dots.
The yellow cross is at [24, 48], spanning rows 13-35 and cols 37-59. To overlap, the blue cross needs to be at a position where its span intersects with this.
The blue cross's span is center_row - 11 to center_row + 11 and center_col - 11 to center_col + 11.
To overlap with the yellow cross's span (rows 13-35, cols 37-59):
- center_row - 11 <= 35 and center_row + 11 >= 13, so 2 <= center_row <= 46.
- center_col - 11 <= 59 and center_col + 11 >= 37, so 26 <= center_col <= 70.
The yellow dots are at [3, 15], [9, 6], [9, 24], [17, 15]. To cover [3, 15], the blue cross needs center_row - 11 <= 3 and center_col - 11 <= 15 <= center_col + 11, so center_row <= 14 and 4 <= center_col <= 26.
But center_col <= 26 and center_col >= 26 means center_col = 26. And center_row <= 14 and center_row >= 2 means 2 <= center_row <= 14.
From [14, 26], the blue cross spans rows 3-25 and cols 15-37. The yellow cross at [24, 48] spans rows 13-35 and cols 37-59. Overlap is rows 13-25 and col 37. So the crosses overlap.
From [14, 26], the blue cross covers:
- [3, 15]: row 3 is within 3-25, col 15 is within 15-37. ✓ (vertical bar at col 26 doesn't cover col 15, horizontal bar at row 14 doesn't cover row 3. Wait, the cross covers cells on its bars, not the entire span.)
Let me reconsider. The cross covers cells on its bars:
- Horizontal bar: row = center_row, cols = center_col - 11 to center_col + 11.
- Vertical bar: col = center_col, rows = center_row - 11 to center_row + 11.
From [14, 26]:
- Horizontal bar: row 14, cols 15-37.
- Vertical bar: col 26, rows 3-25.
The yellow dot at [3, 15] is at row 3, col 15. This is not on the horizontal bar (row 14 ≠ 3) or the vertical bar (col 26 ≠ 15). So it's not covered.
So the blue cross at [14, 26] doesn't cover [3, 15].
To cover [3, 15] with the blue cross:
- Horizontal bar: row = 3, so center_row = 3. Cols = center_col - 11 to center_col + 11. To cover col 15, need 4 <= center_col <= 26.
- Vertical bar: col = 15, so center_col = 15. Rows = center_row - 11 to center_row + 11. To cover row 3, need -8 <= center_row <= 14.
So from [3, 15], the blue cross covers [3, 15] on the horizontal bar.
From [3, 15], the blue cross spans rows -8 to 14 and cols 4-26. The yellow cross at [24, 48] spans rows 13-35 and cols 37-59. No overlap (rows -8 to 14 don't overlap with rows 13-35... wait, row 14 is within 13-35, but cols 4-26 don't overlap with cols 37-59). So no overlap.
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me try a different approach.
